mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Ecuador on?
mapQuestions.answer: South America. Ecuador sits on the Pacific coast in the north-west, sandwiched between Colombia and Peru 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Ecuador?
mapQuestions.answer: Quito, the highest official capital city in the world at 2,850 metres. It sits almost directly on the equator but never gets hot, because of the altitude.

mapQuestions.question: Why is the country called "Ecuador"?
mapQuestions.answer: Because the equator runs straight through it! "Ecuador" is the Spanish word for "equator". You can stand with one foot in each hemisphere at the Mitad del Mundo (Middle of the World) monument near Quito.

mapQuestions.question: Which famous islands belong to Ecuador?
mapQuestions.answer: The Galápagos, 1,000 km out into the Pacific. This is where Charles Darwin worked out his theory of evolution after seeing how each island's animals had adapted differently.

mapQuestions.question: What money does Ecuador use?
mapQuestions.answer: The US dollar! Ecuador switched in 2000 after its own currency, the sucre, lost most of its value. Today it is one of the few countries in the world that uses US dollars as its official currency.

foods.name: Ceviche ecuatoriano
foods.description: Shrimp ceviche with lime, tomato, red onion and coriander, served with crunchy popcorn and plantain chips ON TOP. Different from Peruvian ceviche, and brilliant.

foods.name: Encebollado
foods.description: A strong tomato-and-fish soup with yuca chunks and pickled red onions. Served with popcorn, lime and chilli on the side. The national breakfast and famous restoring dish.

foods.name: Llapingachos
foods.description: Pan-fried mashed-potato-and-cheese cakes, served with peanut sauce, chorizo, fried egg, avocado and salad. The highland Sunday lunch.

foods.name: Hornado
foods.description: A whole pig slow-roasted in a wood oven for hours, then carved and served with mote (boiled white corn), llapingachos and salad. Common in highland markets.

foods.name: Patacones
foods.description: Twice-fried green plantain, smashed flat into thick crispy discs, salty and crunchy. Eaten with cheese, beans, ceviche or just on their own.

foods.name: Ecuadorian chocolate
foods.description: Ecuador grows some of the very best fine cacao on Earth. Pacari, Republica del Cacao and Hacienda San José all make world-class dark chocolate from beans grown a few kilometres from the factory.

scavengerItems.label: A "Latitude 0°0'0" sign or the Mitad del Mundo equator monument
A real iguana or marine iguana (Galápagos!)
An active volcano (Ecuador has dozens, Cotopaxi, Tungurahua, Reventador)
A woman in a richly embroidered shirt with gold beads and a long woven skirt (Otavalo highlands)
A whole field of red roses (Ecuador exports roses worldwide, they grow huge here because of the equatorial sun and altitude)
A real macaw, toucan, or one of Ecuador's 130+ hummingbird species
The cloud forest in Mindo, full of mist, birds and butterflies
A Galápagos penguin (the only penguin found north of the equator anywhere on Earth!)
A baroque Spanish colonial church with carved gold inside (Quito's old town is a UNESCO World Heritage site)
A fresh-caught Pacific blue crab or langoustine at the coast

geoMatches.description: The colonial highland capital, 2,850 m up, dwarfed by the volcanoes around it. The old town was the FIRST UNESCO World Heritage site ever named (in 1978).
13 main islands, 1,000 km off the coast. Sea lions on park benches, marine iguanas spitting salt, Darwin's evolution laboratory.
A highland town with one of the largest indigenous craft markets in South America. Every Saturday the central square fills with textiles, ponchos and woven goods.
Ecuador's eastern slice of the Amazon basin. Pink river dolphins, monkeys, anacondas, and giant kapok trees.
A colonial city in the southern Andes, with cobbled streets, red-tiled roofs, and the workshops where the famous "Panama hat" is actually made. (Spoiler: Panama hats are Ecuadorian!)
An almost perfectly cone-shaped active volcano, 5,897 metres, often visible from the highway out of Quito. One of the tallest active volcanoes in the world.

senses.smell: Roasting cacao on a chocolate farm, fresh roses in a highland greenhouse, salty Pacific breeze, the cool damp moss of the cloud forest, woodsmoke from a market grill in Otavalo…
senses.hear: Spanish with a sing-song highland accent, the calls of a hundred hummingbird species in Mindo, sea lions barking on Galápagos rocks, Andean pan flutes in markets, distant volcano rumblings…
senses.taste: Lime-bright ceviche with crunchy popcorn on top, melt-in-the-mouth 80% Ecuadorian dark chocolate, salty green-plantain crisps, sweet roasted corn nuts, warm potato-and-cheese llapingachos…
senses.feel: Thin equator-mountain air that gives you a headache the first day, hot Galápagos sun, the soft heavy straw of a Panama hat, the cold mist of Cotopaxi on your face, warm tropical Pacific water…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How did 13 islands change science?
stories.body: In 1835, a 26-year-old English naturalist called Charles Darwin sailed into the Galápagos on a ship called the HMS Beagle. He stayed for five weeks. He noticed that the finches on each island had slightly different beaks: some long and sharp for picking insects out of cracks, some short and strong for cracking nuts, some thin and pointed for sipping flowers. He realised that each island had pushed its birds to change shape, generation by generation, to match the food available. Over the next 25 years he worked that idea into a theory: evolution by natural selection. The book he eventually published, "On the Origin of Species" (1859), changed how humans understand life on Earth. And it all started in Ecuador.

stories.question: What can you actually do on the equator line?
stories.body: Just north of Quito is a yellow line painted across a courtyard, marking Latitude 0°0'0". You can stand with one foot in the Northern Hemisphere and one in the Southern. Guides love to tell visitors that water spins one way on one side of the line and the other way on the other side, but actually that's a tourist trick, water doesn't care about being a few metres from the equator. The REAL strange thing is gravity: at the equator you weigh about 0.5% less than you do at the poles, because the Earth bulges out a bit at its waist and the spin makes you feel slightly lighter. It's tiny, but it's real, and pilots and engineers actually have to account for it.

stories.question: Why is the Panama hat actually Ecuadorian?
stories.body: The world-famous "Panama hat" is not from Panama at all. It is woven by hand from the leaves of a palm-like plant called "toquilla" that grows on the Ecuadorian coast. The hats have been woven in Ecuador for hundreds of years. They got their wrong name when the Panama Canal was being built in the early 1900s, thousands of workers there bought them to keep the sun off, photos went around the world, and everyone assumed they were Panamanian. The finest hats (called "Montecristi superfino") are woven so tightly they can hold water and roll up small enough to pass through a wedding ring. A really good one can take six months to weave and cost thousands of dollars.

stories.question: Why does Ecuador have so many volcanoes?
stories.body: Ecuador sits on the "Ring of Fire", where the Nazca tectonic plate (under the Pacific) is being pushed underneath the South American plate. As the Nazca plate dives down it gets crushed and heated, and the rock above melts and rises back up as magma. That magma builds the Andes, and pokes through them as volcanoes. Ecuador has around 30 active volcanoes today, including Cotopaxi (5,897 m), Chimborazo (6,263 m), Tungurahua and Reventador. Chimborazo has a brilliant claim: because of the Earth's equatorial bulge, the top of Chimborazo is the FURTHEST point on the surface of the planet from the centre of the Earth, even though Everest is taller above sea level.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Chimborazo is the closest point on Earth to the moon, even though Everest is "taller". Why do you think we usually measure mountains from sea level instead of from the centre of the Earth?

stories.question: Why is Quito built on top of an Inca city?
stories.body: Long before the Spanish arrived, Quito was an important Inca city in the northern part of their empire. When the Spanish conquered it in 1534, they didn't flatten everything. They built their churches, palaces and cobbled streets directly on top of the Inca foundations. Today Quito's old town is the largest, best-preserved colonial centre in the Americas, with 40 churches, monasteries and convents packed into a few blocks, several covered in carved gold leaf inside. UNESCO named it the first ever World Heritage site in 1978, alongside the Galápagos. So Ecuador got both of the very first two UNESCO sites.

convoQuestions.question: Highlight food of Ecuador so far?
Have you tried saying "qué más, pana?" to anyone? What happened?
Mountains, Galápagos, Amazon, cloud forest, which has felt the most "Ecuador"?
Did you stand on the equator line? Did you feel lighter? (You actually did, by about 0.5%!)
What's the strangest animal you have seen here so far?
Ecuador uses US dollars instead of its own money. How would you feel if your country did that?

wordSearch.hint: Ecuadorian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Giant Galápagos tortoise
animals.description: Can live 150+ years and weigh 250 kg. Wandering free on Santa Cruz and Isabela islands.

animals.name: Marine iguana
animals.description: The only sea-going lizard in the world, only found in the Galápagos. They sneeze salt out of their nostrils.

animals.name: Galápagos sea lion
animals.description: Lying on park benches, beach steps, and harbour walls. Completely unbothered by humans.

animals.name: Blue-footed booby
animals.description: Seabird with extraordinary bright blue feet. Does a high-stepping dance to attract a mate.

animals.name: Galápagos penguin
animals.description: The only penguin found north of the equator. Tiny, fast, and rare.

animals.name: Andean cock-of-the-rock
animals.description: A bright orange Ecuadorian cloud-forest bird, the national bird of Peru too. Loud, weird, hard to forget.
